analogy: Think of "Geomorphic Processes" through the chapter's own opening images: LANDFORMS AND THEIR EVOLUTION A fter weathering processes have had their actions on the earth materials making up the surface of the earth, the geomorphic agents like running water, ground water, wind, glaciers, waves perform erosion. It is already known to you that erosion causes... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Geomorphic Processes is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that lANDFORMS AND THEIR EVOLUTION A fter weathering processes have had their actions on the earth materials making up the surface of the earth, the geomorphic agents like running water, ground water, wind, glaciers, waves perform erosion.

The text explains that it is already known to you that erosion causes changes on the surface of the earth.

The text explains that deposition follows erosion and because of deposition too, changes occur on the surface of the earth.

The text explains that as this chapter deals with landforms and their evolution ‘first’ start with the question, what is a landform?

The text explains that in simple words, small to medium tracts or parcels of the earth’s surface are called landforms.

The text explains that if landform is a small to medium sized part of the surface of the earth, what is a landscape?

The text explains that several related landforms together make up landscapes, (large tracts of earth’s surface).

The text explains that each landform has its own physical shape, size, materials and is a result of the action of certain geomorphic processes and agent(s).

The text explains that actions of most of the geomorphic processes and agents are slow, and hence the results take a long time to take shape.

The text explains that landforms once formed may change in their shape, size and nature slowly or fast due to continued action of geomorphic processes and agents.

The text explains that due to changes in climatic conditions and vertical or horizontal movements of land- masses, either the intensity of processes or the processes themselves might change leading to new modifications in the landforms.

The text explains that evolution here implies stages of transformation of either a part of the earth’s surface from one landform into another or transformation of individual landforms after they are once formed.

The text explains that that means, each and every landform has a history of development and changes through time.

The text explains that a landmass passes through stages of development somewhat comparable to the stages of life — youth, mature and old age.

The text explains that what are the two important aspects of the evolution of landforms?

The text explains that rUNNING WATER In humid regions, which receive heavy rainfall running water is considered the most important of the geomorphic agents in bringing about the degradation of the land surface.

The text explains that because of the sheer friction of the column of flowing water, minor or major quantities of materials from the surface of the land are removed in the direction of flow and gradually small and narrow rills will form.

The text explains that waterfalls and rapids may exist where local hard rock bodies are exposed.

The text explains that valley types depend upon the type and structure of rocks in which they form.

The text explains that for example, canyons commonly form in horizontal bedded sedimentary rocks and gorges form in hard rocks.

The text explains that potholes and Plunge Pools Over the rocky beds of hill-streams more or less circular depressions called potholes form because of stream erosion aided by the abrasion of rock fragments.

The text explains that but very deep and wide meanders can also be found cut in hard rocks.

The text explains that they may be bedrock surfaces without any alluvial cover or alluvial terraces consisting of stream deposits.

The text explains that gROUNDWATER Here the interest is not on groundwater as a resource.

The text explains that this is because of (i) propensity of water flowing over very gentle gradients to work laterally on the banks; (ii) unconsolidated nature of alluvial deposits making up the banks with many irregularities which can be used by water exerting pressure laterally; (iii) coriolis force acting on the fluid water deflecting it like it deflects the wind.

The text explains that fUNDAMENTALS OF PHYSICAL GEOGRAPHY 52 percolates well when the rocks are permeable, thinly bedded and highly jointed and cracked.

The text explains that it is this downward and horizontal movement of water which causes the rocks to erode.

The text explains that that is why, the results of the work of groundwater cannot be seen in all types of rocks.

The text explains that but in rocks like limestones or dolomites rich in calcium carbonate, the surface water as well as groundwater through the chemical process of solution and precipitation deposition develop varieties of landforms.

The text explains that these two processes of solution and precipitation are active in limestones or dolomites occurring either exclusively or interbedded with other rocks.

The text explains that any limestone or dolomitic region showing typical landforms produced by the action of groundwater through the processes of solution and deposition is called Karst topography after the typical topography developed in limestone rocks of Karst region in the Balkans adjacent to Adriatic sea.

The text explains that quite often, sinkholes are covered up with soil mantle and appear as shallow water pools.

The text explains that caves In areas where there are alternating beds of rocks (shales, sandstones, quartzites) with limestones or dolomites in between or in areas where limestones are dense, massive and occurring as thick beds, cave formation is prominent.

The text explains that there can be a maze of caves at different elevations depending upon the limestone beds and intervening rocks.

The text explains that this calcium carbonate is deposited when the water carrying it in solution evaporates or loses its carbon dioxide as it trickles over rough rock surfaces.

The text explains that glaciers can cause significant damage to even un-weathered rocks and can reduce high mountains into low hills and plains.

The text explains that there may be lakes gouged out of rocky floor or formed by debris within the valleys.

The text explains that most of the rock fragments in till are angular to sub-angular in form.

The text explains that some amount of rock debris small enough to be carried by such melt- water streams is washed down and deposited.

The text explains that the rock fragments in outwash deposits are somewhat rounded at their edges.

The text explains that very coarse materials like boulders and blocks along with some minor fractions of rock debris carried into this stream settle in the valley of ice beneath the glacier and after the ice melts can be found as a sinuous ridge called esker.

The text explains that the long axes of drumlins are parallel to the direction of ice movement.

The text explains that the drumlins form due to dumping of rock debris beneath heavily loaded ice through fissures in the glacier.

The text explains that drumlins give an indication of direction of glacier movement.

The text explains that wAVES AND CURRENTS Coastal processes are the most dynamic and hence most destructive.

The text explains that most of the changes along the coasts are accomplished by waves.

The text explains that when waves break, the water is thrown with great force onto the shore, and simultaneously, there is a great churning of sediments on the sea bottom.

The text explains that constant impact of breaking waves drastically affects the coasts.

The text explains that storm waves and tsunami waves can cause far-reaching changes in a short period of time than normal breaking waves.

The text explains that as wave environment changes, the intensity of the force of breaking waves changes.

The text explains that do you know about the generating forces behind waves and currents?

The text explains that (i) What do incised meanders in rocks and meanders in plains of alluvium indicate?

Source: NCERT Class 11 Fundamentals of Physical Geography, Chapter 6: Geomorphic Processes

cases.name: The
cases.point: The characteristics of each of the stages of landscapes developing in running water regimes may be summarised as follows: Youth Streams are few during this stage... (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch6 pg 1)

cases.name: Mature During
cases.point: Mature During this stage streams are plenty with good integration. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch6 pg 1)

cases.name: Old Smaller
cases.point: Old Smaller tributaries during old age are few with gentle gradients. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch6 pg 1)

cases.name: EROSIONAL LANDFORMS Valleys Valleys
cases.point: EROSIONAL LANDFORMS Valleys Valleys start as small and narrow rills; the rills will gradually develop into long and wide gullies; the gullies will further deepen, widen... (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch6 pg 1)

cases.name: Potholes
cases.point: Potholes and Plunge Pools Over the rocky beds of hill-streams more or less circular depressions called potholes form because of stream erosion aided by the abrasion... (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch6 pg 1)

schemes.name: In
schemes.point: In the transportation process sand and silt act as effective tools to abrade the land surface. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch6 pg 12)

prelims_traps: Do not treat LANDFORMS AND THEIR and EVOLUTION as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Geomorphic Processes; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Geomorphic Processes through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use LANDFORMS AND THEIR and EVOLUTION to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
